coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic technology for value added products from feather keratin
coredata.dc:creator: Azmi, Nur Afiqah
Idris, Azila
Yusof, Nor Saadah Mohd
coredata.dc:description: Feather keratin is a biomass generated in excess from various livestock industries. With appropriate processing, it holds potential as a green source for degradable biopolymer that could potentially replace current fossil fuel based materials. Several processing methods have been developed, but the use of ultrasonication has not been explored. In this study, we focus on (i) comparing and optimizing the dissolution process of turkey feather keratin through sonication and conventional processes, and (ii) generating a biodegradable polymer material, as a value added product, from the dissolved keratin that could be used in packaging and other applications. Sonication of feather keratin in pure ionic liquids (ILs) and a mixture containing ILs and different co-solvents was conducted under different applied acoustic power levels. It was found that ultrasonic irradiation significantly improved the rate of dissolution of feather keratin as compared to the conventional method, from about 2 h to less than 20 min. The amount of ILs needed was also reduced by introducing a suitable co-solvent. The keratin was then regenerated, analyzed and characterized using various methods. This material holds the potential to be reused in various appliances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Regeneration
Keratin
Feather
Ionic liquids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Attenuated total Reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR)

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Rate of dissolution of feather keratin in [BMIM]Cl (ultrasound, 200 W of power) and [BMIM]Cl (conventional heating, without sonication).
Rate of dissolution of feather keratin in 2.0 M of [BMIM]Cl/DMSO at applied P = 120 W, P = 200 W, P = 280 W, pure [BMIM]Cl at P = 200 W and conventional method.
Summary of dissolution and regeneration processes of raw turkey feather through ultrasonic-assisted and conventional methods.
ATR-FTIR spectra of (a) raw feather and regenerated keratin materials from 2.0 M of [BMIM]Cl in DMSO (b) P = 120 W, (c) P = 200 W, (d) P = 280 W and (e) 20 g of [BMIM]Cl.
Results from XRD analysis of (a) raw feather and regenerated keratin materials from 2.0 M of [BMIM]Cl in DMSO (b) P = 120 W, (c) P = 200 W, (d) P = 280 W and (e) [BMIM]Cl.
Single heating scan TGA traces of raw feather and regenerated keratin materials after various dissolution conditions.
DSC results of (a) raw feather and regenerated keratin materials from (b) [BMIM]Cl and 2.0 M of [BMIM]Cl in DMSO with (c) P = 120 W, (d) P = 200 W and (e) P = 280 W.
List of reaction medium for ultrasonic reaction.

row.entry: 0.015 M CTAB + 0.015 M sodium salicylate (1:1 ratio, 50 ml), Power: 200 W
0.1 M of CTAB (50 ml), Power: 200 W
0.05 M of CTAB + 0.05 M of sodium salicylate + 0.1 M of propanol (50 ml), Power: 200 W
0.015 M of CTAB + 0.015 M of sodium salicylate + 1 M of propanol (50 ml), Power: 200 W
0.2 M of BMIMCl in DMSO (50 ml), Power: 200 W
0.5 M of BMIMCl in DMSO (50 ml), Power: 120 W
0.5 M of BMIMCl in DMSO (50 ml), Power: 200 W
2.0 M of BMIMCl in DMF (50 ml), Power: 200 W
2.0 M of AMIMCl in DMF (50 ml), Power: 200 W
2.0 M BMIMCl in DMSO (20 ml), Power: 200 W
5.0 M BMIMCl in DMSO (20 ml), Power: 200 W
2.0 M BMIMCl in 2-propanol (20 ml), Power: 200 W

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Time taken for dissolution of 500 mg of feather in 35/65 mixture of [BMIM]Cl/DMSO solvent.

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic technology for value added products from feather keratin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Malaya, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Malaya Centre for Ionic Liquids (UMCiL), University of Malaya, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Malaya Centre for Ionic Liquids (UMCiL)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Feather keratin is a biomass generated in excess from various livestock industries. With appropriate processing, it holds potential as a green source for degradable biopolymer that could potentially replace current fossil fuel based materials. Several processing methods have been developed, but the use of ultrasonication has not been explored. In this study, we focus on (i) comparing and optimizing the dissolution process of turkey feather keratin through sonication and conventional processes, and (ii) generating a biodegradable polymer material, as a value added product, from the dissolved keratin that could be used in packaging and other applications. Sonication of feather keratin in pure ionic liquids (ILs) and a mixture containing ILs and different co-solvents was conducted under different applied acoustic power levels. It was found that ultrasonic irradiation significantly improved the rate of dissolution of feather keratin as compared to the conventional method, from about 2 h to less than 20 min. The amount of ILs needed was also reduced by introducing a suitable co-solvent. The keratin was then regenerated, analyzed and characterized using various methods. This material holds the potential to be reused in various appliances.

ce:section.ce:para: Annually, an estimated 5 million tons of feathers are produced as a by-product of poultry farming from a reliable production pipeline [1], making it a dependable biopolymer feedstock. The development of alternative industrial uses of feather keratin will not only reduce environment impacts and health hazards which occurs when these materials are sent to landfills, but will also increase the value of the feathers [2]. However, feather keratin is usually rendered into a low-grade feed stock or disposed as waste [3]. This is due to the limitation of the keratin fiber in which at the temperatures above their crystalline melting point, concurrent crystalline melting and degradation will destroy the unique protein structure and fibrous nature [4]. The keratin as a natural monomer for use in eco-composites and bio-plastics is one of the appealing end-uses of this type of biopolymer [5].

Feather keratin is made up of ordered α-helix or β-sheet structures with other disordered structures. Feather fiber has a higher percentage of α-helix as compared to β-sheet [6]. The formation of sulfur–sulfur bonds that bond with other cysteine molecules is due to the presence of about 7% cysteine in feather keratin [7]. In its solid state, the strength and stiffness of keratin were contributed by these cysteine linkages (disulfide bridges) [8]. However, keratin is bound by strong internal interactions (non-covalent interactions such as electrostatic forces, hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interaction and covalent interaction such as disulfide bonds) that stabilizes the protein structure which makes it harder to dissolve or to be extracted [9,10]. Hence, it is vital to develop a technique to dissolve keratin and to produce solutions that is industrially scalable, able of re-crosslinked, and have minimal to none destruction of the primary protein chains. Reduction or oxidation reactions are among a wide number of techniques that are available for dissolving hard keratin [11]. Furthermore, the process of cleaving the disulfide bonds with the aid of 2-mercaptoethanol as a supplier of thiols is known as a process of dissolution of keratin without damaging its primary protein chain [12]. However, the fact that large quantity of reagents such as acids or reductants is consumed and cannot be recycled is among a few of the serious shortcomings concerning these methods for extraction of keratin. Thus, finding simple and eco-friendly processing methods to dissolve feather keratin has been a primary focus for researchers. Some recent works have been reported regarding the use of ionic liquids in dissolution and regeneration of keratin fibers [9,13]. Regardless of the higher cost of ionic liquids than that of inorganic reagents, they can be reused and recycled thus improving the productivity of whole process, leading to lowering the overall cost of the process [14].

Ionic liquids (ILs) are salts that are liquid at low temperature (most are liquid at room temperature or below), and are composed wholly of ions in its molten form [15]. ILs consist of positive (cation) and negative (anion) charges, similar to salts that is bonded together by electrostatic interaction. However, in contrary to solid salts that pack neatly together to form a crystalline structure, ILs possess freedom in movement due to their loosely packed ions, thus allowing the salt to take on the property of flow [16]. One of the advantages of ILs is that it has negligible vapor pressures due to its high thermal stability, hence it can withstand high temperature reactions. Since major method of molecular solvent discharge into the environment is through evaporative loss, the use of selected ILs may offer environmental advantages in industrial processes to which they are suitable. Second, a range of substrates can be dissolved by ILs, and a growing number of studies reported that ILs can provide improvements in product yields, selectivity or ease of product recovery when used in place of molecular solvents [15]. In contrast to traditional volatile organic compounds, they are called ‘‘green’’ solvents because of their negligible vapor pressure and recyclable properties [17,18]. The ability to choose the anion or the cation makes large number of combination of salts up to 1018 ILs are theoretically possible [19]. These ions can be combined and functionality can be introduced, thus allowing specifically designed functional ILs [16]. In the literature, one can find a range of ILs and their applications as it can be tailored to exhibit extremes of behavior, i.e., to have functional pharmaceutical therapeutic ability [20,21], to provide support for cells and enzymes [19,22], to be used in drug delivery [23], or to kill microbes [24], designed to be biodegradable [25–27] made from renewable resources such as amino acids [28] or biomass [29], or made to be durable and long lasting, to facilitate the clean-up and removal of environmental pollutants such as carbon dioxide (CO2) [30–32] and for metal extraction [33–35]. However, misuse in any of these applications can lead to the potential environmental damage [36]. ILs can also be hydrophobic, hydrophilic, acidic or basic [16].

Ultrasound has been widely used in various industrial and biomedical applications, especially in diagnostic and therapeutic medicine [37,38]. Sound waves above the frequency of human hearing (≥20 kHz) is referred as ultrasound. Depending on the frequency, ultrasound is divided into three categories, namely power ultrasound (20–100 kHz), high frequency ultrasound (100 kHz−1 MHz), and diagnostic ultrasound (1–500 MHz) [39]. Strong vibration occurs when ultrasound propagates through a medium. Severe structural damage can occur if the sonication medium contains solid particles, for example, plant materials such as seeds. Cleaning and extraction applications is also possible with such mechanical vibration effects. Ultrasound generates acoustic streaming within liquids on top of the mechanical vibration effect [40], which enhances effective mass transfer leading to productivities in some industrial processes. The propagation of ultrasonic waves in liquid medium results in a phenomenon known as acoustic cavitation [41]. Acoustic cavitation occurs when microbubbles in a solution grow with the resonating acoustic waves and collapse after reaching a resonance size range [37]. The near adiabatic collapse of cavitation bubbles result in the generation of very high temperature which in turns aid in the formation of highly reactive radicals. For example, due to high temperature conditions, homolysis of water molecules within bubbles can generates H and HO radicals (generally referred to as primary radicals). Secondary reducing radicals can be produced in the presence of suitable solutes [42].

The main aim of this study was to investigate and compare the possibility of dissolving keratin by combining ultrasound to a current available method which uses ILs. The method optimization involved our attempts to reduce the usage of ILs by mixing ILs with suitable co-solvents and shortening the processing time. A solvent with appropriate boiling point was chosen to ensure that the reaction is viable without degrading the structure of feather keratin. A combination of analytical techniques including ATR-FTIR, PXRD, TGA and DSC was used to investigate the physicochemical properties of the regenerated keratin materials and the degree of feather keratin solubility in these ILs and through the usage of ultrasound. Such a study has never been reported before, and we believe that this could offer a new ultrasonic technology that can be utilized in industrial scale. The use of minimal amount of ILs will also lead to a positive environmental impact as well as reducing the financial and synergic cost of such process. Therefore, we believe that this study will be of great interest to chemists, engineers and environmental scientists.

Cleaned turkey feathers was purchased from Club House, Spotlight, Selangor, Malaysia, and only the barb was used. The ionic liquids chosen is 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride ([BMIM]Cl, 98% purity), purchased from Sigma Aldrich and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Merck.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total Reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR)
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained using a Perkin-Elmer 400 RX1 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The FTIR was performed in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The spectra were recorded with a resolution of 4 cm−1 with 30 scans. Spectra were baseline corrected.

At 22 ± 2 °C using a Panalytical Empyrean powder diffractometer, the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were obtained. Approximately, 1–2 g of the finely ground sample was placed randomly on a locally designed flat brass sample holder for each analysis. CuKα1 radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) was produced at 40 kV and 25 mA. By using a 2θ-range of 5–50.0° (2θ) range, the data were collected in the Highscore Plus (θ/2θ) horizontal geometry using with a step size of 0.02° 2θ and an accompanying scan rate of 0.5° min−1.

For TGA analysis, samples were placed in a flowing nitrogen atmosphere in a Thermogravimetric Analyser, TGA 6 (Perkin Elmer, USA) between 30 and 700 °C at a heating scan rate of 10 °C min−1. This analysis was carried out to investigate the thermal stability of the raw turkey feather and regenerated keratin materials. The samples were first dried under vacuum in an oven at a temperature of 60 °C. Before running each experiment, these samples were then loaded in ceramic pans and equilibrated and started at the starting temperature of 30 °C.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was conducted on a Diamond DSC (Perkin Elmer, USA) with 5–10 mg of sample in closed aluminum pans, at a ramp rate of 10 °C per minute. All samples were analyzed from room temperature to 250 °C, then cooled to 50 °C before heated for the second time to 250 °C. The first heating was done to diminish any thermal history and the second heating was done for analysis of the materials. Transition temperatures are reported using the peak maximum of the thermal transition.

The following equation was used to measure the ultrasonic power dissipated into the reaction liquid; Power (W) = dT/dtCpm, where Cp is the heat capacity of water (4.2 J g−1) and m is the mass of water (g). (dT/dt) is the temperature rise per second. The sample volume was 20 cm3 and the initial temperature rise was measured at room temperature by using a thermometer, which was immersed in the sample solution and held at the half height of the solution.

Three types of solutions were prepared; solution A (0.4 M potassium iodide + 0.05 M sodium hydroxide +1.6 × 10−4 M ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate), solution B (0.1 M potassium hydrogen phthalate) and 20 ml sonicated deionized water. The deionized water was sonicated for a set interval of time and afterward, 1 ml was mixed with each 1 ml of solution A and B. The freshly mixed solution was then irradiated with UV to get an absorbance value at 355 nm.

When an aqueous KI solution is irradiated with ultrasound, I− ions are oxidized to give I2. I2 reacts with the excess I− ion to form
I
3
-
ion when excess
I
-
ions are present in solutions [43]. Concentration of KI was 0.1 mol dm−3. The absorbance of
I
3
-
at 355 nm was measured (ε = 26,303 dm3
mol
- 1
cm
- 1
).

The solubility experiments were conducted in 20 ml beaker. The ultrasound waves were transmitted in the solvent system by a probe transducer at 20 kHz with applied powers of 120 W, 200 W and 280 W by using Branson Sonifier 450. A fixed amount of 500 mg feather keratin was determined for dissolution as a constant throughout the experiment. Small incremental amounts of 50 mg of feather keratin were gradually added to the solvent system to quantify the solubility, and mechanically stirred until they were completely dissolved in the solvent. Initially, it took a few minutes for the dissolution of turkey feather. Then the dissolution rate started to become constant as each batch of feathers was added to the system. The viscosity of the ILs remains unchanged but the color of the solutions becomes darker as time passed. The complete dissolution of the feather was confirmed visually and by lighting the solution from the outside. The dissolution time for each applied power was recorded.

At first, experiments were conducted to compare the efficiency of feather keratin dissolution, particularly the dissolution time, in the absence and presence of ultrasonic irradiation. The experiment was conducted by immersing the probe transducer into a reaction beaker at fixed depth and various power supplied by the ultrasound generator. The process was continued with the sequential addition of feather keratin until all the feather was dissolved completely in the solvent yielding in a viscous solution. No precipitation was observed even after the solution mixture was cooled to room temperature. It is deduced that ILs can dissolve the feather keratin by mainly breaking the hydrogen bonds in keratin fibre. The dissolution is due to the role of chloride and [BMIM]+ ions in ILs that disrupted the hydrogen bonding in the keratin material. The Cl− ions associated with the hydrogen of protein backbone and [BMIM]+ complexes with the protein oxygen. This interaction disrupted hydrogen bonding in feather keratin and led to the dissolution of keratin. Due to the break of hydrogen bonds, the keratin fibre can move freely (dissolved) in ILs. The mechanism of keratin dissolution in pure ILs has been reported in the literature by Chen, et al. [44]. It has been suggested that ILs are suitable for the dissolution of keratin at high temperatures due to the presence of chloride ions in cellulose and other biopolymers [45–48]. In addition, at elevated temperatures, the keratin structure is unfolded [49] allowing a strong interaction between keratin and the ILs as reported by Zhang et al. [9].

The experimental results, shown in Fig. 1 , provide a clear indication that the use of sonication in the dissolution process has significantly shortened the dissolution time of feather keratin. Fig. 1 results compare the time taken for the dissolution of 500 mg of feather keratin in 20 g [BMIM]Cl between sonication and conventional heating method. According to the method in the literature [50], the conventional heating was carried out at 130 °C. This is because at temperature below that, dissolution of feather keratin was found to be less efficient. It has also been reported before that the conventional method of feather keratin dissolution is optimal at 130 °C due to the optimum solubility [9]. Conventional method with heating at higher than 130 °C was found to generate more water soluble peptide and amino acid, thus resulting in lower yield of regenerated materials [51]. However, for sonication method, no temperature control was used.

As can be seen in Fig. 1, conventional heating method required almost 2 h to be completely dissolved. This is due to the strong cuticle structure which protects the cortex, hence retaining the original structure of the feather. However, when a similar experiment was carried out in the presence of sonication, the processing time was reduced by about 90% to 11 min at much lower temperature. The dissolution of cortex happens within the first few minutes of heating, however the complete dissolution of the cuticle takes much longer time.

In the case of sonication, both physical (shear) and chemical (radicals) effects are generated by acoustic cavitation. In order to see which of these effects contribute to the effective dissolution of keratin, further experiments were carried out. In parallel, efforts were taken to reduce the amount of ILs in order to lower the cost of the overall process. For this purpose, attempts to dissolve feather keratin were carried out in different solvents, namely, pure [BMIM]Cl and a mixture of [BMIM]Cl and water, ethanol, propanol, dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (CH3CN) or dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO). [BMIM]Cl was also mixed with an aqueous solution of cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (surfactant) or simple salts such as sodium salicylate. A small amount of feather dissolution was observed in some of the systems, however, a complete dissolution was only achieved with either pure [BMIM]Cl or [BMIM]Cl/DMSO. Further details are provided in the Table 1 below.

Another aspect that has not been discussed yet is the choice of DMSO as a co-solvent (Table 1). Amongst different co-solvents trialled, only DMSO was found as an effective co-solvent for the dissolution process. In order to explain this observation, we considered the physical properties of various solvents used in this study. Among various properties, the vapor pressure of DMSO was found to be much lower than other co-solvents trialled (the vapor pressure of DMSO is 55.6 Pa). It is well known that the collapse intensity of bubbles during acoustic cavitation strongly depends upon the vapor pressure of the solvent. Cavitation in a low vapor pressure solvent would generate stronger physical forces and mass transfer effects. An attempt was also made by carrying out the experiment in only pure DMSO. However, no positive effect was observed. The dissolution efficiency was monitored by mixing DMSO with pure IL at various proportions. At 2.0 M of [BMIM]Cl in DMSO, full dissolution of feather keratin was achieved. This proves that the contribution of ILs is still crucial due to the presence of Cl− ion which plays a major role in disrupting the hydrogen bond in keratin thus making dissolution of keratin with addition of organic solvent plausible. However, after certain optimum concentration, further increase of ILs did not have any significant efficiency improvements on the processing until pure ILs was used. Further studies on acoustic power effect were carried out using [BMIM]Cl in DMSO system.

The data shown in Fig. 2 compares the dissolution efficiency of pure [BMIM]Cl and 35/65 mixture of [BMIM]Cl/DMSO solvents in the presence of ultrasound. It could be seen that the rate of dissolution significantly decreases when pure ILs is replaced with the mixed solvent system. However, this could be considered as a compromise between the rate of dissolution and cost effectiveness. In addition, a reduction in the amount of ILs would also result in lowering environmental impact. Hence, from an industrial application view point, the amount of ILs could be reduced by 65% with a 55% increase in rate of dissolution as compared to the conventional method.

Fig. 2 data also considers the effect of ultrasonic power on the dissolution rate of keratin in pure [BMIM]Cl and 35/65 mixture of [BMIM]Cl/DMSO solvents. This series of experiments were carried out in order to find an optimal power level, again, keeping possible industry applications in mind. In order to provide a clear picture of the power effect, the data presented in Fig. 2 is summarized in Table 2 .

The observed power effect is of interesting and important. It could be seen from Table 2 data that the time taken for the complete dissolution is almost constant (within experimental errors) when the power was varied from 120 W to 280 W. Even at the lowest power level used, the maximum dissolution rate could be achieved. This indicates the possibility that the optimum power applied could be achieved at even lower power. The power effect is also important to understand the possible role of ultrasound in enhancing the dissolution rate.

From calorimetric power data (see Fig. S1), it is clear that the amount of energy delivered to the system increases with an increase in power. From H2O2 yield data (Fig. S2), it is clear that the number of radicals produced increases with an increase in acoustic power. It is known that the radicals generated at 20 kHz is relatively less than higher frequency ultrasound. [41] Combining these observation with that of Table 2, it could be concluded that radicals (chemical effects) do not play significant role in improving the rate of dissolution [52].

It can then be suggested that the physical forces and the mass transfer effects generated during acoustic cavitation are responsible for the observed enhancement in the dissolution efficiency. Also, the mass transfer effects generated at the lowest power (120 W) is more than sufficient to achieve maximum efficiency. Further details on the mechanism are provided below.

Despite the significant improvement of ultrasonic assisted dissolution (UAD) process as compared to the conventional heating method, a concrete mechanism requires an extensive experimentation. First, let us look at the properties of feather keratin. Feather keratin consists of two distinct regions, the sulfur-rich outer layer known as the cuticle and the inner component called the cortex. The cuticle contains numerous disulfide bonds which connect the peptide chains and is composed of three layers, the epicuticle, exocuticle and endocuticle. Of the three layers, the exocuticle has the highest sulfur content. Thus, the mechanism of this dissolution process starts with the swelling of the cuticle followed by swelling of the cortex. Then the cortex starts to dissolve in a few minutes, however confined in the swollen cuticle. The time taken for the dissolution of the cortex is significantly shorter (less than 5 min) than that to completely dissolve the cuticle which can take up to hours. High content of disulfide bonds within the cuticle is the main reason of the extended time required to dissolve the cuticle. The swelling of the keratin during dissolution suggests that the cortex may remain inside the fiber even in the dissolved state. The single fiber in situ observation technique is applied as reported by Chen et al. [44] to prove that indeed the cortex remains inside the casing of the cuticle even in the dissolved state. Finally, the cuticle was dissolved and both cuticle and cortex were dispersed in the solvent system. The disulfide bonds can also be chemically reduced or oxidized through pre-treatment such as using thioglycolic acid [53], or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [54]. After treatment with thioglycolic acid, formation of –SH is achieved through partial reduction of the disulfide bonds. Wool treated with thioglycolic acid was found to dissolve five times faster in ILs compared to wool without any treatment. The overall process; cuticle swelling, cortex swelling, cortex dissolution and finally cuticle dissolution remained the same [53]. With hydrogen peroxide, the wool surface is oxidized. The most noteworthy response to the ILs is obtained in which the cuticle fully swelling in way shorter time than for the raw fiber. Total dissolution time is significantly decreased too. Interestingly, with the aid of hydrogen peroxide, simultaneous dissolution occur for both the cuticle and the cortex [44].

Based on the current data collected, and the fact that the presence of ILs is still crucial in all the dissolution attempts, it is best for now to speculate that the mechanism of ultrasonic assisted dissolution works similar to the conventional heating. However, the shorter processing time required is due to physical forces generated. During bubble oscillation and bubble collapse, microstreaming of the solution is generated, resulting in an increase of mass transfer. At the same time, when the bubble attaches to the cuticle, asymmetric collapse may take place, resulting in a phenomenon known as microjetting that may help weakening the cuticle structure. Once the cuticle is broken at different places, the feather structure could dissolve instantaneously shortening the required time for dissolution.

Despite the ability of ultrasound in speeding up the keratin dissolution process, we are also concerned on preserving the protein backbone of the keratin. To confirm that the protein backbone is preserved, the dissolved keratin in the absence and presence of ultrasound were regenerated, analyzed and compared by different techniques as will be discussed in the next section.

After the dissolution of feather keratin from both procedures, the regeneration of keratin was carried out following a method published in the literature [44]. Fig. 3 shows the processes involved from raw turkey feather to regenerated keratin materials after both, conventional and ultrasonic assisted dissolution process. As mentioned earlier, the ultrasonic dissolution of feather keratin has never been reported before. Therefore, it is crucial to regenerate and characterize the keratin structure to investigate whether its protein backbone is retained or disrupted. The keratin regeneration was carried out by adding water at room temperature, and the dissolved keratin precipitated from the solution immediately through the action of stirring. The regenerated keratin was vacuum dried at 60 °C for 3 days after being separated by centrifugation for 20 min at 4000 rpm. A brown-coloured solid is obtained as the regenerated keratin materials (Fig. 3). This plastic-like material holds the potential for various application as in biomedical and textile industry [55]. These materials were further characterized.

Multiple characterization methods were carried out to determine that the regenerated keratin materials have the similar physical and chemical properties as the raw feather keratin. This is important to ensure that the dissolution through ultrasound process did not affect the properties of keratin.

ATR-FTIR measurement can be used to assess the structural changes in proteins. Hence, the regenerated (water insoluble) keratin materials from the ILs can be further understood through this analysis. Through the infrared absorption spectra, the absorption bands of peptide bonds (–CONH–) are usually the main characteristic bands detected for the regenerated keratin materials. The vibrations in the peptide bonds originate from bands known as Amide A, Amide I, II, and III [56]. From these spectra, the orientation of the constituents and the conformation of the polypeptide chains are the information that will be obtained through this analysis. For raw keratin, due to the N–H stretching or also known as the Amide A vibrations, a medium absorption bands of peptide bonds was observed at 3286 cm−1 [57]. The regenerated keratin materials show identical stretching frequencies (Fig. 4 (b)–(e)) at 3292, 3289, 3385 and 3317 cm−1 respectively. Amide I, the most intense absorption band in proteins, is useful for the analysis of the protein secondary structure and contributed mainly from CO stretching. Strong absorption bands were observed at between 1600 and 1700 cm−1 in both raw feather and regenerated keratin materials. The Amide I peak predominantly consists of peptide carbonyl stretching vibrations of the α-helix (one strong band at 1633–1634 cm−1) and β-sheet (one strong band at 1615–1638 cm−1) [58]. The absorption band in Amide I of regenerated keratins shifted towards a lower frequency were indicative of increased amounts of ordered β- sheet structures [59]. Amide II shows a strong absorption peak at 1532 cm−1 for raw feather and it was attributed to C–N stretching and N–H bending vibrations. The regenerated keratin materials bands were observed at 1538, 1533, 1536 and 1541 cm−1 respectively, similar to the raw feather. The C–N and C–C stretching, N–H plane bending and CO bending vibration resulted in a weak absorption band that was observed at 1220–1300 cm−1 which was due to Amide III for both raw feather and regenerated keratin materials. It can be observed from the spectra that the raw feather and regenerated keratin materials are similar and there are no signatures of new functional groups appearing in the regenerated keratin materials [14,50,51,56–58,60–66].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out for raw feather and regenerated keratin materials. Comparison of the XRD patterns are as shown in Fig. 5 . There are three noteworthy peaks that should be observed for keratin which expressed the crystallinity of the material. A high intensity 2θ peak at about 9° (0.98 nm) and 17.8° (0.51 nm) as shown in these figures has been assigned to α-helix meanwhile typical of the β-sheet structure peak is also at 9° (0.98 nm) and at about 19° (0.47 nm) [67,68]. Hence, these peaks should be taken into consideration during the analysis of the regenerated materials. However, in the regenerated keratin materials (b), (c) and (d), the peak at 9° disappeared which indicates that during the dissolving process, the crystallinity was reduced and is not obtained back during the regeneration process [9,69]. Only regenerated material (e) has the 9° peak but with lower intensity. Based on the spectra the substantial 2θ peak at about 9° (0.98 nm), 17.8° (0.51 nm) and 19° (0.47 nm) represent the crystallinity in the raw feather. However, due to the overlapping signals at about 17.8° and 19° from the α-helix and β-sheet structures, only single peak is obtained as in the spectra due to both the α-helix and β-sheet peak cannot be assigned accurately. From Fig. 5, similar diffraction patterns were obtained for the regenerated keratin materials exhibit, at around 20.3°, 20.2°, 23.2° and 21.7° for (b) to (e) respectively proving that the crystallinity pattern of the regenerated keratin materials is similar as the raw feather. Moreover, broader peaks are observed in all regenerated keratin, which may be due to the overlapping of the β-sheet and β-turn peaks. However, the peaks at about 20° are significantly less in intensity for the regenerated material (b), (c) and (d), suggesting a lower content of the β-sheet structure hence reduced crystallinity. This observation contradicted with material (e) which produced higher intensity peak which resulted higher crystallinity [50,63,64,67,68].

TGA was conducted to analyze the thermal decomposition of the raw feather and regenerated keratin materials (Fig. 6 ). The raw feather and regenerated keratin materials show thermal stability up to more than 200 °C. In all samples, there are two stages of decomposition transitions that can be observed. The first stage occurred at about 100 °C which shows a small weight loss that could be due to the evaporation of residual moisture or solvent that was bound in the regenerated keratin materials. For the second stage, the degradation of the keratin materials occurred between 250 °C and 400 °C. It is understood that the rupture of the helical conformation and disulfide bond breakage is associated with the keratin degradation [50,60,63,70].

The phase behavior of the raw feather and regenerated keratin materials were studied by using differential scanning calorimeter (DSC). Three consecutive heating and cooling cycles were carried out in order to ensure that no moisture was present in the samples and to remove any thermal history for the sample. The typical heat flow curves of the 3rd heating cycle of the DSC traces are as shown in Fig. 7 . An exothermic peak around ∼230 °C is observed for the raw feather as reported by Idris et al. and Wu et al [50,71] which is usually assigned to crystalline melting [60], α-helix denaturation and disordering [72]. However, the DSC traces of regenerated keratin materials show no peaks at the reported area as in Fig. 7.

These results are consistent with the XRD results which shows reduced crystallinity for regenerated keratin materials. No obvious glass transition (Tg) was observed in the raw feather and regenerated keratin materials.

In summary, low frequency high power sonication was found to be effective in dissolving feather keratin by disrupting the hydrogen bonding in the material in ILs and ILs/DMSO solution. Furthermore, ultrasound process reduced the processing time significantly as compared to the conventional method. Through calorimetry and radical calibrations, it was confirmed that the dissolution process is aided mainly by physical (shear) effect upon sonication; chemical (radicals) generated by sonication is minimal and does not affect the protein backbone structure. By ATR-FTIR, XRD, TGA and DSC, it was observed that the protein backbone is still intact and no major chemical changes of the polypeptide chains occur through ultrasonic method. The thermal stability of regenerated keratin materials show improvement comparing to the raw feather despite some changes in structures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the grant BK036-2015 and FS-UMRG-KIMIA-17 provided by University of Malaya for funding this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.04.016.

ce:section.ce:para:
